lines.text: Section seven. Biosensors — engineering biology to detect.
Living cells are remarkable detectors.
Cellular processes rely on the specific recognition of molecules — small metabolites, nucleic acids, lipids, proteins — and that recognition can be repurposed for sensing.
Biotech has long exploited this principle.
Antibody-based assays like ELISA — the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay — use the specific binding of antibodies to detect biomolecules in research and clinical samples.
ELISA-style tests are commercially ubiquitous, ranging from pregnancy tests to disease diagnostics to environmental monitoring.
Synthetic biology aims to make biosensor design as rapid and predictable as electronic sensor design.
The vision is a platform technology: specify what molecule you want to detect, get back an engineered cell or device that detects it sensitively and specifically.
The biggest commercial success in biosensors is the blood glucose monitor — used by millions of diabetics every day to manage their blood sugar.
But glucose monitors are dedicated, single-molecule devices.
Each one detects only one compound, and the development cost per molecule is high.
What synthetic biology aspires to provide is a programmable biosensor platform — one that can be retargeted to any analyte by swapping in a different sensing module.

The architecture would include a recognition module (a protein, RNA, or DNA that binds the target molecule), a transduction module (something that converts the binding event into a detectable signal), and an output module (a reporter that produces a visible, measurable readout).
Each module would be standardised, characterised, and composable.
The reality is that biosensor engineering remains largely bespoke.
Each new analyte requires its own discovery work to find a suitable recognition element, its own optimisation to couple recognition to readout, and its own characterisation campaign.
But there is progress.
Genetically encoded biosensors — built into living cells — have been demonstrated for many target compounds: heavy metals, sugars, signalling molecules, antibiotics, even cancer biomarkers.
In the laboratory, these biosensors detect their targets with high sensitivity and specificity.
Scaling them to deployable products — robust to handling, storage, and varied use environments — is harder, and most engineered biosensors are still in research rather than commercial stages.
The longer-term vision is more ambitious.
The explosion of genomic and proteomic data in clinical research has given us thousands of disease biomarkers — proteins or RNAs whose levels indicate disease state.
A general-purpose biosensor platform that could be rapidly tailored to any biomarker would enable personalised medicine: diagnostics on demand, customised to each patient's biology.

That platform does not yet exist, but synthetic biology research is moving toward it, and the supporting technologies — cheap DNA synthesis, characterised parts, computational design — are improving every year.
